did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used transfer learning with a pre-trained AlexNet deep convolutional neural network to classify skin lesions into seven classes (melanoma, melanocytic nevus, basal cell carcinoma, actinic keratosis, benign keratosis, dermatofibroma, and vascular lesion) using the ISIC 2018 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a method for skin lesion classification using transfer learning with AlexNet on the ISIC 2018 dataset. It includes specific sections for methods, experiments, results (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision), and a conclusion, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Classification of Skin Lesions into Seven Classes Using Transfer Learning with AlexNet
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of accurately classifying skin lesions into seven specific classes (melanoma, melanocytic nevus, basal cell carcinoma, actinic keratosis, benign keratosis, dermatofibroma, and vascular lesion) to aid in early melanoma detection. The authors proposed a method utilizing transfer learning with a pre-trained AlexNet deep convolutional neural network, where the original model's parameters served as initial values and the last three layers were replaced and randomly initialized. The model was trained and evaluated using the ISIC 2018 dataset, which comprised 10,015 dermoscopic images across the seven classes. The evaluation approach involved computing accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and precision, with experiments demonstrating that data augmentation helped balance class imbalances and improve performance. The proposed method achieved final performance metrics of 98.70% accuracy, 95.60% sensitivity, 99.27% specificity, and 95.06% precision. The results indicate that the proposed AlexNet-based transfer learning approach outperformed existing state-of-the-art methods by at least 6% in accuracy.

all_task_types.target: Seven discrete skin lesion classes (melanoma, melanocytic nevus, basal cell carcinoma, actinic keratosis, benign keratosis, dermatofibroma, and vascular lesion)
all_task_types.details: Multiclass classification of skin lesions into seven specific diagnostic categories using a pre-trained AlexNet deep convolutional neural network with transfer learning on the ISIC 2018 dataset. The model predicts discrete class labels, evaluated via accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and precision.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) 2018 challenge, consisting of dermoscopic images of skin lesions.

use.training: Used for training the modified AlexNet model with transfer learning. In Experiment 1, 10-fold cross-validation was used. In Experiments 2 and 3, the dataset was divided into 80% for training and 20% for testing.
use.testing: Used for testing the model performance. In Experiment 1, 10-fold cross-validation was used. In Experiments 2 and 3, 20% of the dataset was used for testing.
use.other: Used for comparison with state-of-the-art methods.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used in the first experiment to divide the ISIC 2018 dataset into groups for training and testing without augmentation, ensuring each group was used at least once as training and testing.
Used in the second and third experiments to divide the augmented dataset into 80% for training and 20% for testing.
Applied to overcome class imbalance and prevent bias in the classifier by expanding the number of images for underrepresented classes.
Used to compute and visualize performance metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision) for each experiment.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and precision metrics derived from confusion matrices, and compared qualitatively with state-of-the-art methods.

Experiment 1.description: Original ISIC 2018 images without augmentation; 10-fold cross-validation used.
Experiment 1.notes: Low sensitivity and precision due to class imbalance and insufficient images in some classes (VASC, DF, AKIEC, BCC).

Experiment 2.description: Data augmentation applied to six classes (except N.V.) to balance class distribution; 80% training / 20% testing split.
Experiment 2.notes: Data augmentation improved classification rates by balancing class weights.

Experiment 3.description: Data augmentation applied to all classes except N.V. with random rotation and flipping; 80% training / 20% testing split.
Experiment 3.notes: Final reported results. The method outperformed state-of-the-art methods by at least 6% in accuracy.

Comparative Study.description: Comparison with state-of-the-art methods on the ISIC 2018 dataset.
Comparative Study.notes: The proposed method exceeded the performance of existing classification methods.

architecture_details.modification: The last three layers of the original AlexNet were dropped out and replaced with new layers for seven-class classification.
architecture_details.frozen_layers: The rest of the network parameters (pre-trained layers) remained unchanged from the original model.

classification_or_output_head.type: Fully connected layer + Softmax + Classification layer

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation via random rotation (angles from 0° to 355°) and flipping for six classes (excluding Melanocytic Nevus) to balance class distribution.
Data augmentation applied to all classes except Melanocytic Nevus in the third experiment using random rotation and flipping.
No preprocessing applied to the original ISIC 2018 images in the first experiment (10-fold cross-validation).

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to images with random rotation angles ranging from 0° to 355°.
Applied alongside random rotation to augment images.

Data augmentation was applied to six classes (Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis, Benign Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, and Vascular Lesion) to balance class distribution. These classes were augmented to have approximately 6700 images each (±5), matching the size of the largest class (Melanocytic Nevus, which had 6705 images and was not augmented). This was done to overcome class imbalance and prevent classifier bias.

conclusions: The proposed method utilizing transfer learning with a pre-trained AlexNet network successfully classified skin lesions into seven classes (melanoma, melanocytic nevus, basal cell carcinoma, actinic keratosis, benign keratosis, dermatofibroma, and vascular lesion) with high accuracy.
The final model achieved performance metrics of 98.70% accuracy, 95.60% sensitivity, 99.27% specificity, and 95.06% precision on the ISIC 2018 dataset.
Data augmentation was critical for overcoming class imbalance issues inherent in the ISIC 2018 dataset, significantly improving classification rates compared to experiments without augmentation.

The proposed AlexNet-based transfer learning approach outperformed existing state-of-the-art methods by at least 6% in accuracy when compared to results from Brinker et al., Gessert et al., and Hardie et al. on the same dataset.

The study demonstrates that deep convolutional neural networks, specifically AlexNet modified with transfer learning, are effective for multi-class skin lesion classification, addressing challenges such as visual similarity between lesions and class imbalance.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the ISIC 2018 dataset, which exhibits significant class imbalance (e.g., Vascular Lesion has only 142 images vs. Melanocytic Nevus with 6705), potentially biasing results if not properly addressed.

Data augmentation was critical to performance; the first experiment without augmentation yielded low sensitivity (82.17%) and precision (82.17%), while the final experiment with augmentation achieved 95.60% sensitivity and 95.60% precision.

The Melanocytic Nevus class was not augmented because it already had a large number of images (6705), while other classes were augmented to approximately 6700 images to balance the dataset, which may not reflect real-world clinical prevalence.
The model was trained and tested on a single dataset without external validation, limiting the generalizability of the results to other populations or imaging devices.
The hardware used for training was a consumer-grade NVIDIA GeForce MX150 GPU, which may limit the scalability or reproducibility of the training process for larger datasets.

Comparisons with state-of-the-art methods (Brinker et al., Gessert et al., Hardie et al.) are based on reported metrics from other papers rather than re-running their models on the same data, which may introduce inconsistencies in evaluation protocols.
The paper does not report statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values) for the performance differences between the proposed method and comparative methods.

The proposed method achieved 98.70% accuracy, which is significantly higher than comparative methods (81.5% to 93.01%), but this gap may be partly due to the extensive data balancing strategy applied only to the proposed method.
